sentences.text: I was small boy.
And I looked at the relationship.
My father loved my mother like crazy.
He was-- Maybe he would resist, you know, something.
But in the long run, he always did what my mother said.
I remember one time when they quarreled [? once, ?] and he left-- left the the home.
And I was following him.
He was smoking like this near the river.
I sat beside him, held his hand.
He liked to play with my hand, always to make it like-- and I just wanted him to hold me stronger.
I said to him, so what-- what's going on to be?
Are you coming to Israel or not?
He said, I don't know.
You're going to listen to-- to Mother or not?
He said, I don't know.
But the way he said it, I said, he will listen very-- He will listen, and my mother, when we came back, she opened the door.
It was ready, the food and all this.
So they do tomorrow, you have to make-- you have to make ready the horses because we're going to Warsaw.
From Warsaw, we're going to-- and he said, I heard it already.
But he did it.
So it was not a question of, OK, I want to convince you.
He knew that she's doing well.
But he also knew that he's going to leave my sister, and the children, and all of this, and the community, and the rabbi.
And it was very difficult for him.
And that's the reason that he died very young, I think, about 55, 56.
I don't know exactly, but he was very young.
And he died very shortly after it.
He was a man that he loved children.
He had a lot of humor.
My mother also, very humoristic.
And he-- he couldn't believe when they told him that she died, and the children will never see.
He died very shortly.
I don't remember ever that he was ill.
And he died.
So he accepted it.
And-- but in his heart, he never-- never accepted it.
So outside, there was no talking about it.
Also talk about my sister, and the Holocaust, and all this that I think I always left.
I think also my brother.
And I-- When I lecture, I always give, as I said to you, the advice to the-- to the-- to the teachers not to mention too much Holocaust because the children did not understand Holocaust.
It brings them only to fear.
Tell the stories.
Tell about [?
Yitzhakeleh. ?] Tell about children.
Tell about Anne Frank.
Tell the stories.
But when you frighten them, put them-- start with a story.
When you frighten them, Holocaust, they run away.
And that's what happened to me.
That's why later, I have to do some extra.
Maybe talk about it that I will-- what a shame, that I don't know the story of my-- of my sister, my relatives.
I even didn't look at the paintings because of fear as a child.
That-- I don't know whether fear is didn't want to admit that it happened.
Didn't want to admit that we let it happen.
And I think what we felt in my family, in our small, that we are responsible, not protecting these people who stayed behind us.
It's not fair.
But we-- I remember on the ship, when I was there, I saw Yitzach and all of my friends, I said, I'll never see you again.
He gave me this knife.
The first time he gave me his knife, and he said, you make the sculptures.
I said, we are going to see each other.
He said, oh no.
Because the atmosphere was like this.
Even being children, we understand no way we're going to meet each other.
First to teach me.
He was really a very talented.
You know, in-- in this kind of state, in places they grown talent, like playing in the-- my father played, you know, the violin.
And [?
Yitzhakeleh ?] played the violin.
And nobody studied-- teached him too much.
But they've been excellent in it.
And he was sculptor because he-- we read a lot of the Bible and all that.
Made his sculptures from the trees.
You know, he cut trees and all.
It doesn't cost money.
And he got a knife.
And I said to him, how do you do it?
I said, how do you know how to do it?
He said, look I read the Bible.
And I see the trees.
And one day, he never gave me his knife.
He said, you cut yourself.
No way I'm going to give you.
OK, so I looked at the trees, and he said to me, you see here?
Here's the head.
Here are the hands and all this.
And suddenly, I see through the imagination.
These sculptures from the trees, that they how-- how many creatures you can see.
And it helped me a lot.
And the knife, and from that time, my imagination through these few sentences grew from time to time.
And I can see.
And even I-- I try to imagine these things, and my imagination, still now-- till now I paint through the imagination and not through what I see.
I can see.
And after a week, I see you.
After a week, I will come back.
And through my imagination, with the memory of you, I'll make the painting.
That's what the first teacher of mine, the small boy with the knife.
As a child, you know, the mother said, oh you paint wonderfully and all this thing.
But the really-- the true start of my painting was when I was about 16 years old.
I paint much more stronger.
And I made a lot of drawings.
A lot-- all the drawings I-- I sold.
I don't know about 300, 400 drawings.
All are sold around the world.
Yes.
My-- My parents had been very occupied because of this kind of work.
They started 4 o'clock in the morning, when I slept.
They came back at 10 o'clock or 11 o'clock in the evening when I slept.
You know, it was very busy people to make their earnings.
And-- So I had a lot of time to be in the streets.
Until now, I like to be in the streets.
I like to watch people.
If I go even to football game, I look more at the people, how they react, than I took at the football.
Because I like to see the reactions.
I never-- and when I'm here-- I've been-- I've been mugged three times here.
I lost one time $11,000.
I've been mugged really in the streets.
And 142nd Street, you know, on the 8th-- 8th-- on the 42nd on the 8th, I was mugged again.
Not important there, mugging.
And I lost some money and all this.
But what is it?
I like to see the streets, not the 5th Avenue. 5th Avenue I already know.
I like to see the people who emerge into the night.
Who are the people?
The other-- other-- other people, not on the 5th Avenue.
[INAUDIBLE] in the-- how-- in-- in the small places.
I walk a lot.
All-- all my life, I walk all over the world.
Whenever I've been in the night, I just come out about a few hours.
I wanted to see.
I don't mind danger.
No danger, I do it.
That's why I was mugged three times here in New York.
And I still like New York.
And-- and you-- you were running around the streets of Haifa as a child.
Haifa, yes.
What-- what-- what did I do?
We-- we have a-- first thing, all my life, I like movies.
So the first thing, we didn't have money.
So we have to climb when they-- you know, it was open movies, you know, with no roof.
And that was really then, I don't know how we stayed alive.
Because we-- we ran on a-- you know, on old buildings to see the movie, with no-- not paying money.
I lost my head.
I lost this.
I lost that.
So I-- that's one of things.
The second thing is, you know, like every child, stealing small things, you know, chocolate and all this.
And one-- I tell-- did I tell in my book the accident with the chocolate?
This woman, OK, so we have big sticks.
And that also helped us, you know, in-- to have some kind of inventions.
How to do this and that.
That's why I was so good later in the secret service because I had a lot of experience of the street.
I had the atmosphere of the streets.
Big stick with a nail, I took-- put in the chocolate.
And one day, I was caught by a woman.
And it was crazy.
When she met-- caught me, she said, why do you have to steal?
I give you the chocolate, as much as you want.
I have no children.
You know, I'm survivor, she said.
I'd be happy to give you the chocolate.
Don't steal from me.
I came back to the head of-- the head of this gang.
And I said to him, she wants to give me the chocolate.
Don't have to steal it.
He said, oh no.
That's not good.
You have to try it again.
We don't take just chocolate like this.
We have to steal it.
That is-- you have to prove it that you steal.
I said, no way.
And that's maybe the first time-- the first time that-- I-- I didn't sleep the whole night because for me, the head of the gang was very important.
Because it wasn't-- it wasn't straight all the time what I'm going to do.
He wouldn't talk to me.
The gang wouldn't accept me.
And I said tell him-- I said to him-- I came back in the morning.
And I made the decision.
I start to talk to him.
He said, I said you don't talk to me.
I said, no, no, no no.
One thing, I was much stronger than him.
I said to him one thing, you don't tell me when to talk to you.
It's only-- you have only a permission not to listen to me.
But to talk, it's my privilege.
You're not going to shut me up.
That maybe it was the first sign of myself, to look into me.
I don't--
And that maybe is important for the Eichmann story.
I don't take all this-- I don't take all this for granted.
And that is-- is the first time that I check myself.
What does it mean, he doesn't tell me not to talk?
I'm talking as-- as much as I want.
That was the first thing.
Second thing, also, and I remember step by step how I built my character.
Because in the street, you had a lot of problems.
Oh, you become-- you become-- you can be a thief.
You can be-- how you say-- easily, you can be a thief.
You can be a gangster.
You can be everything.
And the question is, what kind of power you have from the other side?
And thanks God my family was helping me.
And my brother Jacob, the-- that I was going on a rope.
And what side I will fall?
Because many-- many became thief.
Now, I don't know what.
And you're alone in the street.
Second thing, we'd be quarreling.
Who's [? koka? ?] Is this [? koka ?] yours?
What is this [? koka? ?] Who gave the [? koka ?] willingly?
Give them what-- this is a thing that you can buy.
So step by step, I-- I-- I would say, I built my-- my character.
I would say.
And I felt that I'm satisfied for myself that I-- I don't accept what is-- how they say-- the surrounding dictate me.
It has to be need to do it.
If I want to take something, I take it.
I don't want, nobody's going to tell me.
And that the street sometimes is so strong that no-- no teacher can teach you.
The other thing was, in school maybe, when I was in school.
And at that time, they beat you.
You know.
One time, I remember that my-- I say-- my teacher brought me, with another guy, into the room.
And he took off his trousers and started beating.
It was the second floor.
I said, I-- I-- I took a stick.
I hang myself.
He said, what are you doing?
I said, I'm going to jump.
I'm not going-- you're not going to beat me.
He said, what?
I'm going to jump.
I-- I promise you, I swear.
I don't believe in your swearing.
And we-- and I jumped, jumped the window.
And I had the feeling being-- I remember-- I had this feeling that I can fly.
Nobody-- nothing can help-- I can fly.
And I jumped.
All my life, I jumped through windows, through this, two stories.
And then I liked-- I believed that maybe, I can fly.
So I jumped this window, two stories.
My-- He was crazy.
And I ran to my-- didn't happen to me.
Yeah, injured here or there.
My mother came with me, back.
He had a small beard, and she hold him like this.
Look, rabbi, if you will ever touch my son, I will kill you.
My mother always, when I had a problem at school, she-- she always protects me.
Never happened that she would say to the teacher, you're right and I wrong.
So that was-- I-- so it shows you that I did-- it was very difficult for me.
And also then when I went to-- into the secret service, that I accept.
Even my most important head of secret service, say something that I can't accept it.
I can't live with it.
Not that I don't want.
I can't live with it, and I rejected it.
It was my teacher.
I show you first was my gang.
Here, my teacher.
I didn't accept-- how you say-- somebody will do to me that I believe it's unjustified.
And my character was like this that I didn't accept, and I rejected it on this way or that way.
That gives you just kind of [? stones. ?] And your-- your father, it seems, picked up on those character traits rather early.
I remember in the book you said you said to your father, what will we do?
Do you remember?
What will we do if we go--
Go to Israel.
I was sure that my mother's going-- is going--
And he said to you don't--
--to do exactly what my father said.
I didn't like it too much.
I wanted my father to be stronger, to reject it.
But I couldn't.
You know.
I'm not-- you know-- in-- in a way, I-- I have feeling for my father that he all the time loved my mother, so much.
That even when he thinks he's right, he would say, OK, I do it.
But he said to you, as a young boy, he said, don't worry.
He said, you're braver than you think.
Oh yes.
Many times he said you're brave.
And many times later, a lot of people said and you crazy.
[LAUGHS]
My mother was running around like crazy.
And my father too, and everybody-- to get the certificate to bring her over.
And what she said is it's not the English.
It's not the Germans.
It is the Jews that didn't let her.
I said, what do you mean?
My-- She said, there's a big line.
And-- And I don't have the connections that I can bring my-- my daughter and the small children.
Even my crying, even my trying, nothing will happen.
Not in my power to bring them.
She blamed a lot the Jewish agency that they-- the priorities were not right.
And read the other book.
When I heard this, I said, look every mother said, you know, that her children are first.
When I read the book about who was first, there been a lot of people that been decided, you know, who will be first by-- by people that didn't have the authority to do it.
And the first time, when after she died, when I read this book, I say mother, you have been right.
I'm not sure that it was right, not to give these children to come first.
I believe in, and I say it now, children are always the first, not important professors or this or that.
What's most important is the innocent.
They didn't try life.
They didn't do anything.
That was not exactly done, as I know now.
So after that, my mother, I couldn't tell her she'd been right.
Because I always looked at it like a mother said.
And then it was just too late to be able to get them out.
Too late, too late.
So.
She was very angry also in her-- in her husband that we didn't know exactly that suddenly he was saved, and they hadn't been.
And we didn't like it.
Yes.
He came later somehow, was saved.
We-- He talked to us and all this.
And I talked to him also when I was older.
I didn't like it.
But you can't-- you can't blame.
You don't know all the details.
You never know how it was-- it was so unpredicted how it would work, being separated.
You know.
When she didn't write anymore, we didn't hear from her, we-- we understood that she's dead.
It never-- There's no way.
You know, for years and years, you don't hear anything.
But the real thing, I've heard, is-- is here in the United States.
That's really kind of something that you couldn't believe it.

I-- I-- when I-- all the story, when it was closed and all this, and captured Eichmann, everything and all this, also finished about 30 years that I can write my book, I said I have to know one thing.
Where we lived, I couldn't remember the name, Zolkiewka, because if I would hear it, I would know.
Nobody could remember.
There was nobody to ask because everybody was dead.
And also, I didn't know the fate of my sister, Fruma.
Dedicated her to the book.
And suddenly, one day in-- near the Lexington Hotel, maybe, you know, it's in the 48th Street.
The was with me a professor, Chaim Shaket, from Miami, he sits-- he's standing right there.
I throw my jacket.
We have to go on a shuttle to-- to Washington.
I threw my jacket in-- into the car, and said, let's take this car.
He said, why this car?
I said, I don't know, I throw my jacket in.
I went to the Lexington, up to the hotel.
Brought my-- my suitcase.
So then you see, it's always written the names of the-- of the driver.
I see a Jewish name, Abraham, or something like this.
I said to him, you Jewish?
He said, you don't see my name there?
I said, yes.
Maybe we talked like this, and I say, you-- you're familiar maybe with the name [?
Milchman? ?] You know, our first name?
He said what do you mean familiar?
I tell you where they are.
They are in Brazil one.
One in Haifa.
One in Tel Aviv.
He knew everything.
And about your, about-- they know who I am.
You know.
And about Fruma, the-- the woman that she died.
She died.
And you-- and the children, that wasn't the same camp.
He-- he hears it, you know, the guy, the professor.
He's still in Miami.
He said, couldn't believe it.
He couldn't believe.
How could it be?
And said to me, I was with her, and I-- I saw how-- how she died, was separated.
And they went to the gas chambers.
He give me the description of her.
And he said, you know, I was there in the camp.
And all-- I-- I-- I succeeded to escape.
I succeed to escape.
And I [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] am now on my way, in Paris.
I was in Paris, on my way to New York.
And-- but-- but before, I-- I decided to go into the camp to see my-- where I lived.
I want to remember.
On my way, I find a small piece of wood.
It's written on it, 'We are three sisters.
Tomorrow we're going to die.
Revenge, revenge, revenge.'
I hold this piece of wood with me.
I said, what kind of revenge it can have?
And suddenly, I hear, in Paris, I hear that Eichmann was captured by the Israelis.
I say, this is my revenge.
I have my revenge.
The first time I felt I can go to New York in peace-- peace of mind.
The professor, I'm going to tell him who is it.
I'm going to tell him that you are the man who captured him, physically captured him.
I said, you're not going to tell him any word.
Because I felt, suddenly like I feel when they talked about the Holocaust, when they started describing all of this.
I said, I don't want anymore this time.
I left.
And I said, I'm going-- I'm going to tell him.
I left to the shuttle.
I already see-- I see the taxi staying there.
And suddenly he starts shouting, I want to talk to you!
I want to talk to you!
I said-- I say, no!
He didn't take from me money.
And I made this mistake that I didn't talk to him because after all, he knew so much.
And this was kind of reaction that I understand it now.
And I said to people, whenever you see a yellow taxi driver from Zolkiewka, tell me.
I'll meet him again.
Till now, nobody did it.
In my lectures, I always ask.
That's was the first time that really, through a yellow taxi driver in New York, I-- I knew what happened to my sister, And how she died and all this.
And that's the story.
In Auschwitz.
Yeah.
I wanted to-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
Is that the clock?
[INAUDIBLE]
